meta.title: The Blackwood Inheritance
meta.premise: A stormy night, a locked manor, and violence at the foot of the stairs. The family was already at each other’s throats.
meta.tone: gothic manor whodunit, tense and formal
meta.artStyle: Victorian gothic oil portrait, head-and-shoulders, candlelit manor palette (deep umber, blood maroon, cold grey-blue), muted desaturated tones, soft Rembrandt lighting, same painted frame and brush style for every cast member, no modern clothing, mystery game character portrait, consistent artist hand across the set
meta.setting: Blackwood Manor, on a storm-cut country road. Night. No one leaves until the weather breaks.
meta.summary: Mr. Blackwood is dead at the foot of the main staircase — a crash, a body, and a household that cannot hide how much they hated him. The storm has sealed the road. Butler Henshaw, the widowed Mrs. Blackwood, daughter Clara, and family solicitor Mr. Vale are all still inside. Someone among them struck the blow. The truth is already written; your job is to find it before morning invents a quieter story.
meta.theMystery: Who killed Mr. Blackwood in his own house, and can you name the killer with method and motive before the storm lifts?

player.role: Police inspector summoned to Blackwood Manor after the death
player.appearance: Weathered face, grey at the temples, steady eyes that miss little
player.clothing: Dark wool coat still damp from the rain, notebook in the breast pocket
player.background: A competent county inspector known for closing messy household cases without scandal — when he can.
player.publicPerception: An unwelcome official with a badge; the household must tolerate him until the storm lifts.
player.voiceNotes: Professional, direct, observant; dry under pressure
player.performanceNotes: NPCs call him Inspector. Staff may be formally cooperative; family may be cold. He has no friends in this house yet.
player.startingKnowledge: You were summoned after a crash and a body. Mr. Blackwood lies dead at the foot of the main stairs. The storm has cut the road; no one left the grounds after ten. Henshaw waits in the entrance hall with the body. Mr. Vale is in the library. Mrs. Blackwood and Miss Clara are in the conservatory. Henshaw has put you in a small guest room upstairs — a place to leave your coat and notebook.
player.objective: Investigate the manor, question everyone still on the grounds, gather evidence, and formally accuse the killer with enough of the how and why to hold.

sections.heading: The summons
sections.text: A telephone call before the storm took the line: Mr. Blackwood is dead at the foot of his own staircase. A crash was heard; the body was already cold when found. The road behind you is washed out. You are the only official presence until morning.

sections.heading: The household
sections.text: Butler Henshaw, who waits with the body. Mrs. Blackwood, the widow. Miss Clara Blackwood, the daughter. Mr. Vale, the family solicitor, staying as a guest. No one has left the grounds since ten o'clock.

sections.heading: The house
sections.text: The entrance hall holds the body and a shattered crystal vase. Mr. Vale is in the library, through the east door. Mrs. Blackwood and Miss Clara keep to the conservatory. Henshaw has put your coat and notebook in a small guest room upstairs.

sections.heading: Your task
sections.text: Question everyone, gather what the house will give you, and be ready to accuse — with the how and the why. A wrong accusation has consequences of its own; the household is watching you as closely as you watch them.

openingNarration: The manor looms against the storm. Inside, the entrance hall smells of wax and old money. A crystal vase lies shattered on the marble. Rainwater pools near the east door. Mr. Blackwood lies at the foot of the main stairs, already cold. Butler Henshaw stands alone with you at the body — pale, formal, waiting for your questions. Mrs. Blackwood and Miss Clara are elsewhere in the house; Mr. Vale the guest as well. The road out is impassable. No one is coming. You were summoned to find who among them did this — and prove it.

phases.description: First look at the hall and household

wrapUp.performanceNotes: After judgment: interactive household aftermath. RETURN TO NORMAL — roads will open, custody for the guilty, the estate leaves crisis mode. Confession, rage, logistics of holding the culprit until magistrates. Do not invent unfound proof on lucky path.

flags.description: Player found the muddy boot print
Someone searched the inspector's guest room

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the entrance hall
locations.description: Marble floor, storm-lashed windows, a stopped grandfather clock, and a shattered crystal vase. The east door leads toward the library wing. Stairs rise to the private rooms.

exits.label: stairs up to your guest room

onInspect.narrativeHints: Shards scatter outward as if struck from above. A black thread snags on jagged glass. Near the east door, a wet size-eleven boot print points toward the library — not staff shoes.
Stopped at eleven. Glass face undamaged. Someone may have stopped it deliberately — or the crash did it.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the library
locations.description: Cold and dark. A fire smolders. A ledger lies open on the desk to tonight's date. Ash in the hearth glints with metal.

onInspect.narrativeHints: A brass key glints in the ash — still warm enough that it was dropped tonight.
The brass key turns. Inside: a letter signed by Mr. Vale dated yesterday: 'If you expose me, I will have no choice.'
Tonight's date shows a large cash withdrawal annotated only as 'settlement — V.'

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the conservatory
locations.description: Glass walls drum with rain. Ferns drip. Far from the east door and the crash.

inspectables.name: floor tiles near the outer glass

onInspect.narrativeHints: Dry enough that no one has recently come in from the storm this way. Weak alibi for anyone claiming to have been outside here all evening.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — your guest room
locations.description: A spare room at the top of the stairs: narrow bed, washstand, a small desk with your coat over the chair. The window faces the storm. It is the only private place Henshaw offered you in this house.

exits.label: stairs down to the entrance hall

onInspect.narrativeHints: Your case notes sit on the desk if you left them. The lock on the desk drawer is ornamental — anyone could open it.
The latch is simple. From outside the roof is too steep; entry would be from the hall door.

relationships.label: long service; protects the household's name
relationships.notes: Henshaw will shield the family until evidence forces his hand.

relationships.label: dislikes Vale as a guest of her husband
relationships.notes: Cool toward Vale; not necessarily accusing.

relationships.label: financial entanglement with the Blackwood name
relationships.notes: Private: Vale's fraud/settlement pressure ran through the late Mr. Blackwood. Do not confess casually.

relationships.label: wary of what the butler may have seen
relationships.notes: Private behavior: sharp when Henshaw is believed.

relationships.label: uneasy around the house guest
relationships.notes: May open up after the letter is known.

characters.name: Mr. Blackwood
characters.shortBio: The master of the house — found dead at the foot of the main stairs.

knowledge.public: Deceased. Found at the foot of the stairs after a crash.

characters.name: Butler Henshaw
characters.shortBio: Long-serving butler; formal, careful, protective of the household's image.

knowledge.public: He heard a crash just after the clock struck eleven. He was in the pantry. When he arrived, the east door stood open and Mr. Blackwood was at the top of the stairs — already falling or already down.

private.content: Only Mrs. Blackwood, Miss Clara, Mr. Vale the guest, and Henshaw himself were in the house.

secrets.content: Henshaw saw Mr. Vale near the east corridor before eleven, not in the conservatory. He is reluctant to accuse a guest without proof.

defenses: Protects the family's reputation unless pressed with evidence

characters.name: Mr. Vale
characters.shortBio: House guest with business ties to Mr. Blackwood.

knowledge.public: Claims he was in the conservatory all evening. Calls any letter a 'business disagreement.'

private.content: He argued with Mr. Blackwood yesterday about money and exposure of a fraud.

defenses: Insists on conservatory alibi until evidence breaks it

characters.name: Mrs. Blackwood

knowledge.public: She was upstairs when she heard the crash. She came down after Henshaw shouted.

private.content: Her shoes are spotted with rainwater though her gloves are dry — she stepped near the east door after the rain blew in, not necessarily outside.

secrets.content: She knew Vale owed her husband and feared scandal, but she did not kill him.

defenses: Will not confess to a murder she did not commit

knowledge.public: She was writing letters earlier. She liked her father more than she liked Vale.

private.content: She overheard her father and Vale arguing about 'exposure' yesterday afternoon.

evidence.name: black thread
evidence.description: A single black thread snagged on the broken vase — matches a dark coat, not staff livery.

evidence.name: muddy boot-print
evidence.description: A man's size-eleven wet boot print near the east door, pointing toward the library. Not polished staff shoes.

evidence.name: brass key
evidence.description: A small brass key recovered from the library hearth ash.

evidence.name: Vale's letter
evidence.description: Letter signed by Mr. Vale, dated yesterday: 'If you expose me, I will have no choice.'

timeline.event: Vale and Blackwood quarrel over fraud exposure and a settlement.
Vale confronts Blackwood in the entrance hall; struggle; vase shatters; Blackwood falls at the stairs.
Vale flees toward the library, drops desk key in the hearth, claims conservatory alibi.
Henshaw hears the crash; body found; storm has cut the road.

canon.notes: Sealed crime history — never send to performer/director packs.

beats.title: The print points east
beats.narrationHints: The boot print is a direction as much as a clue — toward the library wing.

beats.title: The letter changes the house
beats.narrationHints: With the letter in hand, the household's careful manners start to crack. Bonds strain: Vale's money trouble is no longer abstract; Clara's unease with him may show. No relationship menus — show it in looks and dialogue.

reactions.lineHint: If you have that letter, sir… I can no longer pretend I saw nothing.

beats.title: Vale's alibi breaks

effects.text: Vale's composure snaps. He shoves you hard into the banister — a real blow to the shoulder, not a gesture. Pain blooms. Henshaw is close enough to see it.
Before you can step clear, Vale has your arm — a hard grip that does not let you walk away. You are held.
The confrontation spills into the entrance hall. Whether you meant to or not, you are there with him under the broken clock — the house has closed the distance.

beats.narrationHints: Faced with his own hand on the page, Vale's conservatory story collapses into anger. He does not only speak — he shoves you (bruised) and holds you (control: held). You are in the entrance hall with him (and Henshaw nearby). Stage the shove and grip as events that happen TO the player. They cannot calmly leave. Limited window before he acts again.

reactions.lineHint: That letter proves a quarrel — not a killing. Push me again and you will regret how far this goes.

beats.title: Someone searched your room

effects.text: The desk drawers hang open. Your coat has been pulled from the chair and searched. Papers on the desk are scattered; someone was looking for what you know. The door was left ajar.
Seeing your private room torn open hits you in the gut — this is no longer a polite inquiry. Someone has put their hands through your things.

After the search, something you were carrying is gone — lifted from a pocket or taken from the desk. The letter, if you still hold it, is what they wanted most and may still be on you; lesser proof is missing.
My guest room was searched while I was downstairs. Someone in this house is tracking the investigation — and may have taken something from me.
A servant finds you — or Henshaw does — and insists you come upstairs at once. You are brought to your guest room. The door stands ajar. Someone has been through your things.

beats.narrationHints: THIS HAPPENS TO THE PLAYER NOW: they are taken to the guest room, see it ransacked, feel shaken, and may discover something stolen from them (see justHappened stolen_*). Desk open, coat searched, papers scattered. Henshaw may be present, shaken. Do not invent who searched it. The violation must land as an event this turn — not a vague chill for later.

beats.title: Henshaw refuses further help
beats.narrationHints: Accusing the butler freezes the staff. Henshaw will not be drawn out again. Mrs. Blackwood's manners turn to ice.

beats.title: Midnight
beats.narrationHints: The long-case clock should be striking twelve — and thunder answers from the grounds. The night tightens around the house.

beats.title: Birds from the east wing
beats.narrationHints: A black cloud of birds erupts from the east wing, as if something moved where nothing should.

beats.title: Vale strikes

effects.text: Vale does not speak first. The blow lands hard enough to drop you. The manor floor rises. This is how the case ends for you.
The lights go out. You are knocked unconscious — the investigation ends with you on the cold marble.

beats.narrationHints: You waited too long after cornering Vale. He acts first — real violence, knock-out (unconscious), incapacitated. This is a failure ending — murdered. Perform the closing, do not continue the case.

reactions.lineHint: You should have left that letter alone.

beats.title: Vale strikes a warning blow

effects.text: Vale does not wait for the magistrates. He strikes you — a short, vicious blow. Blood on your lip. This is a warning, not yet the end.
The blow puts you on the floor of the entrance hall. You are down — conscious, tasting copper, looking up at him.
In the scramble his hand finds your pocket. Something you carried is gone — not the letter if you still have it, but lesser proof he can make vanish.
The struggle leaves you on the floor of the entrance hall under the broken clock, with Vale still close.
Vale assaulted me and put me on the floor. He is no longer content to threaten. I must finish this before he finishes me.

beats.narrationHints: WARNING ASSAULT (not yet death): Vale injures the player, knocks them down (control: downed), and may steal a pocket item. Stage the blow, the fall, blood, and theft as events this turn. They cannot simply walk away. The retaliation clock is still running — urgency, not ending.

reactions.lineHint: Next time I will not stop at a warning. Leave the letter out of this.

beats.title: Dawn and the empty road
beats.narrationHints: After midnight the case slips away: Vale is gone when the roads begin to open. Failure by time. Perform the closing.

beats.title: The household turns on the detective

effects.text: The household is complaining about my methods. If I do not close this cleanly, I may be the one in cuffs.

beats.narrationHints: Pressure from the family and the ransacked guest room have drawn official eyes. A professional-inquiry clock is running — finish the case or be relieved and detained for overreach.

beats.title: You are relieved of the case
beats.narrationHints: The inquiry lands. You are ordered off the estate, suspended, and detained pending review. Failure by overreach/arrest. Enter wrap-up if enabled.

beats.title: Vale under judgment

effects.text: Judgment breaks the grip. Whether Henshaw steps in or Vale's will collapses, you are free of his hands.
Judgment entered. Vale remains under my eye until the roads open. The household is gathering in the hall.

beats.narrationHints: Aftermath of a correct (or partial) solve: Vale is broken and may confess in dialogue. Household gathers in the entrance hall. Still interactive — player can question him, speak to others, or leave.

reactions.lineHint: You have it. The quarrel — the hall — I never meant… God help me.
Then keep him here until morning. I will not thank you.

beats.title: Wrong name hangs in the air

effects.text: The scene ends in embarrassment rather than a fight. Any grip on you is gone — you are free to leave in disgrace.
I named the wrong person. The house has closed ranks. The real trail cools while I stand here.

beats.narrationHints: Wrong accusation aftermath: the household freezes you out. Vale (if present) may look almost relieved. You are no longer held. Interactive but chilly — player can try to salvage dignity or leave.

reactions.lineHint: You have made a spectacle of yourself. We are finished answering questions tonight.

beats.title: Silence after violence
beats.narrationHints: If wrap-up runs after a murdered failure, keep it brief and dark — aftermath of the detective's fate, not a fresh investigation. Prefer short remaining turns.

beats.title: Henshaw forces Vale to let go

effects.text: Henshaw steps in — firm, formal, and final. He hauls Vale off you or helps you up from the floor. 'That is enough, Mr. Vale.' You are free of his hands for now.
Henshaw forced Vale off me. The household will not yet allow an open assault on an inspector — but the warning is clear.

beats.narrationHints: Henshaw intervenes and RELEASES the player (control → free). Stage the break of grip or help rising from the floor. Player may still be bruised/injured/threatened.

reactions.lineHint: That is enough, Mr. Vale. The Inspector is here under the Crown's authority.
For now. He will not keep waving that letter forever.

beats.title: Vale will not let you wander free

effects.text: You do not make it far. Vale intercepts you — or the household steers you — back toward the entrance hall. The investigation is no longer a tour of rooms; it is a trap closing.

beats.narrationHints: The player is forced back to the entrance hall with Vale. Physical interception, not only dialogue. Make them feel the house choosing where they stand.

reactions.lineHint: We are not finished. You do not get to poke through this house like a free man.

solution.summary: Mr. Vale killed Mr. Blackwood during a confrontation in the entrance hall after Blackwood threatened to expose Vale's fraud. The vase shattered in the struggle. Vale fled toward the library, attempted to destroy papers, dropped the desk key in the hearth, and later claimed a conservatory alibi.
solution.method: Blow during a struggle in the entrance hall; Blackwood fell at the stairs
solution.motive: Silence Blackwood before exposure of financial fraud

requiredFacts.description: Identifies Mr. Vale as the killer
Motive related to exposure / letter / fraud / money
Violence in the hall / struggle / stairs

endings.title: Case closed
endings.templateNotes: Vale is held for the magistrates once the roads open. The letter, the boot print, and the broken alibi seal it — work you actually did. RETURN TO NORMAL: custody is arranged; the household is no longer under his shadow; the storm finally begins to ease. Mrs. Blackwood does not thank you. Outside, authority is coming with the weather.

endings.title: A shot in the dark that hit
endings.templateNotes: You named Vale before the file was complete — little or no hard evidence in hand. Still, the truth is the truth: he breaks. Color drains from his face; the conservatory story collapses into a raw admission. RETURN TO NORMAL: he is held until the roads open; the household can breathe. Do not invent that the player waved evidence they never found. Proof can follow for the magistrates; tonight the killer is unmasked.

endings.title: Thin file
endings.templateNotes: You have part of it — enough pressure to rattle the house, not enough for a clean conviction. Vale twists what he can. Thin RETURN TO NORMAL: he is watched, not free to vanish, but the file is incomplete and the storm is not finished with this story.

endings.title: Wrong man
endings.templateNotes: You commit to the wrong name — or a theory the evidence cannot carry. The real trail cools while the household rearranges its silences. Your superiors will not call this a success.

endings.title: Out of time
endings.templateNotes: After midnight the storm loosens its grip on the road. By the time you can act, Vale is gone — and with him the only living thread that still needed cutting. The case becomes someone else's cold file.

endings.title: You were too late
endings.templateNotes: You pressed Vale without finishing the case. In the dark of the manor he finished it for you. There will be no report in your hand — only another body on Blackwood marble, and a storm that finally ends for everyone else.

endings.title: Relieved of duty
endings.templateNotes: You pushed too hard in the wrong direction. Mrs. Blackwood's complaint and your own overreach catch up with you: you are ordered from the estate, suspended, and detained for inquiry. The murder remains unsolved under your watch.
